query: I've got a tempotron capacity experiment in experiments/capacity.py — run it across seeds 1 through 10 and alpha 0.1, 0.2, 0.5 and tell me where the capacity breaks down. it'll probably need a GPU box
kick off experiments/train.py on a remote 4090 with a 30 minute cap — I don't want it running forever racking up vast charges
run my experiment and keep an eye on the loss as it trains; if it's clearly diverging after a few hundred steps just kill it, no point burning compute
I need to reproduce the run from last week with seed 42 exactly — same commit, same config. how do I do that in this setup?
sweep the learning rate over 1e-4, 1e-3, 1e-2 and batch size 32, 64 for experiments/mnist.py, wait for them all, then tell me which config won
submit this training job so it survives me closing my laptop, and let me know when it's done with the final metrics
my `lab wait` came back with exit code 3 and one job shows teardown_status failed — am I being billed for a machine right now?? what do I do
I want to run experiments/example_capacity.py but it imports scipy which isn't in our locked env, and I don't want to add it permanently — just for this one run
fan out about 50 runs of the perceptron capacity experiment over different K values on vast and pull all the artifacts back when they finish
launch the training job and stream me the metrics live, then grab result.json and the plots once it converges
I keep hearing this is embarrassingly parallel over seeds — what's the cheapest way to actually run a few hundred of these capacity trials without babysitting each one?
read runs/20260601-a1b2c3/output/result.json and tell me what the final accuracy was
write me a tempotron capacity experiment script that logs loss per epoch and writes a result.json at the end
explain what perceptron storage capacity (alpha_c) means and why it's around 2 for the simple perceptron
experiments/capacity.py is really slow on the inner matmul — can you profile it and speed up the numpy?
set up a github actions workflow that runs pytest on every push to main
I need a throwaway Ubuntu 22.04 box with a GPU to mess around in for an afternoon — spin one up and give me SSH access
make a matplotlib chart from these three metrics.jsonl files comparing the sweeps, save it as compare.png
review my PR that refactors the skypilot teardown logic in src/lab/backends/skypilot.py
the mlflow tracking UI won't start on port 5000, I get a connection refused — how do I fix it?
